steps.annotation: This is the source file - text the compiler will read
Program entry point - the CPU starts running here
CPU executes this instruction - prints Hello
Program ends - CPU stops running these instructions
CPU reserves memory and stores 3 in a
CPU stores 4 in b
CPU adds and prints 7

code:
// terminal session
$ g++ hello.cpp -o hello
$ ./hello
Hello
teachMe: g++ compiles hello.cpp into an executable. ./hello runs it. The program prints Hello to the terminal.
When the .exe runs, the CPU executes each line of main() in order. cout prints Run.

clozeSentence: The CPU starts executing at the ___ function
explanation: Every C++ program begins running at main(). The CPU follows instructions inside main from top to bottom.

spec: Write a complete C++ program that prints Run to the terminal.
explanation: Includes iostream, declares main, prints Run, returns 0. The CPU runs each line in order.

question: Type the minimal C++ program that runs and exits cleanly

steps.code: // sum.cpp - this is plain text the compiler reads
steps.annotation: Source code is text - you type it in any editor
A line of text - the compiler reads it as a directive
Each statement is a text instruction the compiler parses
Text instruction telling CPU what to compute and print
Instruction 1: declare x with value 10
Instruction 2: declare y with value 4
Instruction 3: print x

teachMe: max.cpp is text. The compiler reads the text and turns it into a runnable program. Running prints Max: 10.
cat prints the file contents - it's all text. Source code is just text humans write and the compiler reads.

clozeSentence: Source code is just text - type ___ to load the I/O library
explanation: The whole source file is plain text. #include is a text directive the compiler reads.

spec: Write a complete C++ source file (max.cpp) that prints the number 25 to the terminal.
explanation: Source code is text instructions. The compiler reads this text and produces an executable.

question: Type a one-line program that prints OK using cout

steps.annotation: Source file ends with .cpp - this is what you type code into
First non-comment line in any .cpp file is usually #include
Every .cpp executable file has int main()

code:
// terminal
$ ls
error_demo.cpp
$ g++ error_demo.cpp -o error_demo
$ ./error_demo
Test
teachMe: Source code lives in .cpp files. ls shows error_demo.cpp. g++ compiles it. ./error_demo runs and prints Test.
runtime_demo.cpp is a source file. When compiled and run it prints 100.

clozeSentence: C++ source files end with the ___ extension
explanation: C++ source files use the .cpp extension. The compiler g++ expects .cpp input.

question: Type a comment showing the canonical filename for a hello world C++ file, then the include line
code:
// File: hello.cpp
#include <iostream>

steps.annotation: First you write text in a .cpp file
g++ is the compiler - turns .cpp into an executable
Run the executable
Pipeline: text source becomes binary executable
After compilation this line runs as machine code

code:
// terminal
$ ls
syntax_err.cpp
$ g++ syntax_err.cpp -o syntax_err
$ ls
syntax_err.cpp  syntax_err
teachMe: Before compile: only .cpp file. After g++ runs: both .cpp and the new executable exist.

code:
// terminal
$ g++ syntax_err.cpp
$ ls
a.out  syntax_err.cpp
teachMe: Without -o flag, g++ creates default a.out executable. Use -o to name it.

code: $ ___ syntax_err.cpp -o syntax_err
clozeSentence: The C++ compiler is invoked with ___
explanation: g++ is the GNU C++ compiler. It reads .cpp source and produces an executable.

question: Type the canonical g++ compile command for a file named program.cpp producing executable program
code: g++ program.cpp -o program

steps.annotation: When the exe runs, this is the first instruction CPU executes
Second instruction executes after the first
Last instruction - tells OS the program is done
Step 1: n becomes 0
Step 2: n becomes 1
Step 3: n becomes 2
Step 4: prints 2

teachMe: Running the .exe executes each cout in order. A prints first, then B, then C.
CPU executes line 4 (n=5), then line 5 (n*=2 makes n=10), then line 6 prints 10.

code:
$ ___runtime_prog
Line 1
clozeSentence: On Linux/Mac, run an executable in the current directory with ___
explanation: ./ tells the shell to run the executable in the current directory. Without ./ the shell only searches PATH.

question: Type the command to run an executable named myapp in the current directory
code: ./myapp

steps.annotation: cout writes Status: OK to terminal stdout
Second cout call - second line in terminal

teachMe: cout sends text to the terminal. Each cout statement appends to the terminal output.
cout writes Done to terminal then endl ends the line.

clozeSentence: To send output to the terminal, use ___
explanation: cout is the standard output stream. It sends characters to the terminal.

spec: Write a main() function body that prints two lines: Status and the value 0 on separate lines.
explanation: Two cout statements with endl produce two separate lines in the terminal.

question: Type a single cout statement that prints Hello followed by a newline
code: cout << "Hello" << endl;

steps.annotation: Prompt user with text - no endl so cursor stays on same line
Program pauses - user types value, presses Enter, value goes into code
Echo back what user typed

teachMe: User types 7 at the terminal. cin reads it into n. cout prints n+1 = 8.
Program prints prompt, waits for input. User types 42, cin reads it, cout echoes 42 on next line.

code:
int x;
___ >> x;
clozeSentence: To read what the user types in the terminal, use ___
explanation: cin is the standard input stream. It reads from what the user types in the terminal.

question: Type a cin statement that reads an int variable named exitCode
code: cin >> exitCode;
